Chapter 7: A Polarity Flip


The next morning at her lab, Varyn wasted no time. Her fingers flew across her comm-screen, initiating a series of quick vid-chats with colleagues in various scientific disciplines. She was a master of leveraging professional networks, and by midday, she had secured a verified address for Zandil’s residence in the industrial sector. Before she sent it to Corvin, she made one more quick vid-call home, her face beaming onto the living area’s main display.


“Good news, my loves! I’ve located our eccentric genius. It seems he’s quite well-known for his... unconventional methods, even if his funding sources are less so. I’ve sent the coordinates to your wrist-comm, Corvin.”


Corvin grinned. “Excellent work, Varyn. We’ll head out now. Keep us updated on anything else you dig up.”


“Will do. Be careful, both of you. The industrial sector isn’t exactly known for its... pristine pathways.” She gave them a warm smile before her image dissolved.


Soon after, Kael and Corvin were loaded into the family transport, with Kael lying in the back under a blanket. The city’s gleaming core blurs past their viewports. The journey was long, taking them away from the vibrant residential and scientific districts and into the outer rings of the metropolis. When they disembarked, the air immediately felt different. It was dirtier, thicker, and carrying a faint, musty scent of disuse.


Here, towering, skeletal structures of long-shuttered warehouses and defunct refineries loomed, their metal husks corroding, a silent testament to a bygone era of pollution and heavy industry. Most everything was quiet and still, broken only by the occasional hum from a solitary mech repair shop or the low glow of an odd second-hand device store. The roads were cracked, the air heavy with the ghosts of forgotten industry. They had to walk the rest of the way.


Finally, they reached the address Varyn had given them. Before them, a sprawling, ancient house stood defiantly amidst the dead factories. It was Zandil’s home, an anomaly of domesticity, albeit old and broken, right in the middle of all that silent, decaying industry.


Kael and Corvin approached the house confidently. Corvin lifted his hand and rapped sharply on the heavy, metal-plated door. The sound echoed, swallowed by the still, musty air of the industrial sector.


No answer. He knocked again, and then again, harder this time. Still nothing.


Finally, Kael stepped forward. Taking a deep breath, he called out, his voice clear and projecting, “Professor Zandil! My father and I are here to speak with you! We’re incredibly impressed with your discovery of quantum entanglement! You must be a truly rare genius!” He paused, then added, “We’re humble inventors ourselves.” Kael winked at his father. “And we have an idea we hope could build directly off your amazing discovery!”


A flurry of metallic clicks and heavy thuds sounded from within, followed by the clatter of a chain. The door wedged open, revealing Zandil. He squinted at them through the narrow gap, his eyes suspicious, his wild hair standing at all angles.


“Alright,” Zandil rasped, his voice low and gravelly. “What do you mean by idea?” He spat the last word out in mild contempt.


“Could we come in?” Corvin asked, stepping slightly forward.


“Not until you tell me what idea,” Zandil insisted, his gaze darting between them, assessing.


Suddenly, a sleek, silver arm gently but firmly pushed Zandil aside. Trindl appeared in the doorway, his ferrofluid face bearing its characteristic wide, welcoming smile. “Nonsense, Zandil. You stop being rude, you old grump.” He turned back to Corvin. “Please, come in, come in. Would you like some tea? I am Trindl.”


Disarmed by Trindl’s unexpected appearance and hospitality, Kael and Corvin stepped inside. They found themselves in a sparsely decorated living area. A synth-leather couch, designed for two or three people, faced a large video screen, currently dark. But the rest of the room was dominated by mountains of stacked boxes, overflowing chests, and unruly piles of paper. The walls, even the ceiling in places, were covered in erasable boards, each crammed with a chaotic sprawl of equations, diagrams, and theoretical musings in various colors of phosphorescent ink.


Only a small, circular table by a dusty window offered a clear space. On it, a single, life-like holographic image shimmered: a woman with a gentle smile, holding a small girl, perhaps nine years old, in her arms, who looked up at her mother with adoration. The image moved subtly, like most pictures, preserving a ghost of a memory.


Corvin wasted no time. “Professor Zandil,” he began, leaning slightly forward on the synth-leather couch, “we know you’re a busy man, so we’ll get right to the point. My name is Corvin Solan, son of Bitron. This is my son Kael Solan, son of Tira.”


Zandil and Trindl shot glances at each other. The very person they needed to meet had just shown up at their door.


Zandil leaned forward, squinting at Kael, his eyes narrowing as he took in the young man's smooth face and slightly shorter ear tips.


“Solan, son of Tira,” he rasped, his eyes darting to Kael's hands. “You look barely old enough to wire a basic logic circuit. I was expecting a seasoned engineer, not a... post-adolescent apprentice.” He spat the last word out in mild contempt.


“He’s your son? Is that right?” Zandil looked Kael up and down. “How old are you, boy?”


“I’m 17, sir.”


“Yeah, funny thing about that. I happen to know all the children who were born in the last cycle. Not personally, mind you. It’s just something I keep track of for the sake of resource and population figures. The whole, burning ball of death in our sky and whatnot makes me anxious.”


Corvin stepped up, “I assure you, Professor, we had all the proper papers to have a child.” This was true. They did have permission to have a baby, just not this baby.


“Well,” Zandil sniffed and said, waving a dismissive hand, “if your idea is sound, your age is irrelevant. Genius is immune to wrinkles or puberty. Proceed.” he crossed his arms and sat down hard into the old couch. A halo of dust mites jumped up and surrounded his body.


Corvin and Kael then took turns speaking and laid out their idea with clear, concise language, explaining the concept of leveraging quantum entanglement not just for state correlation, but for the instantaneous transfer of information and data. Kael, for his part, carefully omitted any mention of the otherworldly inspiration or the sensation of being slapped into awareness. It sounded crazy even to him.


Zandil’s initial suspicion melted away, replaced by a slow-dawning comprehension, then a flicker of pure, unadulterated excitement. His eyes, already wild, seemed to ignite. Before Corvin could finish, Zandil sprang from his seat, nearly tripping over a stack of forgotten schematics.


“How did I not think of that!?” he practically shrieked, his voice filled with a giddy delight. He paced frantically, hands already gesticulating. “How wonderful! The inherent non-locality… the collapse of the wave function… of course! Do you have any idea how to accomplish this? Do you have a prototype? Any preliminary notes? Diagrams? Equations?” His questions tumbled out in a rapid-fire barrage.


“No,” Kael said, a faint smile touching his lips at Zandil’s effusive reaction. “We don’t have any of that yet. Only the idea. But we do know where to begin.”


Zandil stopped pacing, his gaze fixed on Kael, a brilliant, almost childlike grin spreading across his face, his arms spread wide in excitement. “Then let’s not waste another moment! Follow me to my lab!”


He sprinted towards a heavily fortified door at the far end of the living area, pulling at a series of complex latches. Throughout his excited outburst, Zandil remained utterly friendly and chipper. Secretly, he was elated to have company. And this was not just any company, but minds capable of grasping his genius, even adding to it. And with Corvin’s support and assistance with the shield wall technology, he could stabilize the particles so they could last longer than a few sparse seconds.


Trindl watched the interaction, his smile softening as he observed the nascent camaraderie. He quietly retrieved the nutrient paste tube Zandil had abandoned from the floor and followed the two newcomers and his partner towards the lab. He was happy to see the man he loved smile and be social with other people, but it’s not healthy to hermit away the way he does forever.


Their initial weeks were a whirlwind of theoretical discussions, discarded equations, and spectacular failures. Eventually, Corvin had been able to acquire the necessary components from an old decommissioned generator bunker on the far side of the dome. But they still had been unable to maintain the connection between the two ions for more than a second. Corvin and Kael ended up spending several nights a week at Zandil’s house; the extra torpor pods left behind by his family kept everyone rested and refreshed.


Nights bled into days, often unnoticed, fueled by synthetic nutrients delivered patiently by Trindl, and Zandil’s own boundless, erratic energy. Trindl, ever the anchor, managed not only their nutritional needs but also the increasingly complex organization of experimental data, the precise calibration of salvaged components, and the often-frayed emotional equilibrium of his brilliant, erratic partner. He was the quiet constant amidst the storm of genius, his blue optical sensors reflecting every triumph and every setback with unwavering focus.


The air in the lab was thick with the smoke of spent electricity, the acrid scent of overheating components, and the occasional frustrated swear. Prototype after prototype of the Chroniton Inducer and its peripheral devices warped, fizzled, or simply refused to stay engaged for any significant amount of time.


There were days of shouting matches, Zandil threatening to abandon the whole “sun-burnt endeavor” for a new, even wilder tangent, only for Kael to calmly point out a subtle flaw in his reasoning or Corvin to sketch a more stable power conduit. Each failure brought a fresh wave of despair, only to be met by Zandil’s unshakeable belief, Corvin’s methodical problem-solving, or Kael’s quiet, almost instinctive nudges toward unexpected solutions.


Slowly, painstakingly, order began to emerge from the chaos. The “awareness” flashes Kael experienced became more frequent and intensely focused, guiding his intuition towards solutions that defied conventional logic. He’d feel a prickling sensation behind his eyes, a sudden, overwhelming surge of information, and then he would simply know which discarded circuit board contained the exact resonance dampener they needed, or how to subtly adjust a crystal’s angle to achieve a more stable photon split. These were moments of profound clarity amidst the pervasive frustration, validating his unique, almost psychic, connection to technology. They were exhausting, leaving him drained but exhilarated, always pushing him forward.


Three months later, the core principle was finally sound. They could maintain the entanglement between two of the particles indefinitely. Moving states and charges back and forth between them as many times as they wanted, without any noticeable degradation in connection or stability.


The next step was to switch from elementary condition states to attempt to send a small amount of data, a one or a zero, across the gap between ions.


Now that the shield technology provided the temporal stability required to maintain the entangled pair, the focus shifted from duration to manipulation. The challenge was imposing a clear, binary signal onto the delicate quantum link. The most practical solution involved adapting nanoweave technology, the same material that stabilized the city shield, to create twin, ultra-stable quantum regulators. These regulators would use a precise electromagnetic pulse from the Chroniton Inducer to force the entangled particle into a specific charge polarization. They established a clear protocol: a positive charge polarization would represent the binary ‘one,’ and the instantaneous, correlated negative charge polarization on the remote particle would represent the binary ‘zero.’ This precise forcing of the quantum state, protected by the nanoweave regulator, was the breakthrough that allowed the instantaneous, directional transfer of information.


They had successfully transferred not just a particle’s state, but an encoded single bit of data from one QEO to another, virtually light-years away in theoretical space, through nothing but their instantaneous link. The moment a single character, the letter ‘A’, appeared simultaneously on two screens in separate, shielded chambers, one in the lab, one set up remotely in a rarely used supply closet, Zandil let out a triumphant cheer that echoed through the industrial district, startling a flock of dormant mech-avians from the rafters. Corvin simply nodded, a rare, profound smile spreading across his face, his usual stoicism replaced by undeniable pride.


Kael felt a deep, quiet satisfaction, the kind that resonated in his bones, a hum of accomplishment that settled deep within him.


But a single bit was a far cry from anything remotely useful. The next few months were dedicated to scaling up the process and acquiring specialized components. This proved to be its own unique challenge, often requiring Corvin’s discrete connections to navigate Veridia’s tightly controlled resource allocation.


While Zandil could scavenge much of the core infrastructure, the incredibly precise nonlinear crystals and bespoke energy converters needed for higher data densities were rare, often needing to be custom-fabricated or acquired through less conventional, and sometimes not strictly legal, private markets. Zandil’s lab became a bizarre labyrinth of gleaming, custom-made quantum resonators interspersed with his usual repurposed junk, a testament to their blended ingenuity and resourcefulness.


During this grueling period, Kael turned eighteen years old. His ear tips had reached a respectable length, and he looked and felt the part of an adult man. But he did not celebrate; instead, he spent countless hours refining the Ion-Reader, the elegant interface device that translated raw data into the subtle quantum manipulations needed for transfer, and then read the instantaneous results. He meticulously coded and re-coded its algorithms, adjusting, optimizing, and endlessly testing. It was a complex symphony of sensors and processors, tuned to detect the minute positive charge applied to one QEO and the instantly correlated negative charge on its twin. Kael obsessed over its efficiency, its speed, its capacity to translate the incredibly subtle “language” of entangled particles into coherent, usable information. He often worked late into the day, long after his father had retired, driven by an inner fire that made sleep seem like a waste of precious time.


Finally, after months of relentless refinement, the prototypes were ready. Two sleek, briefcase-sized devices, their polished weave-steel shells hiding the intricate quantum mechanisms within.


They could now transmit an entire textbook’s worth of data in the blink of an eye, powered by nothing more than a small energy pulse to initiate the link. They demonstrated not just text and images, but complex schematics, 3D models, and even perfectly replicated spoken words, streamed instantly and clearly across any distance within their simulated parameters. The team ran every conceivable test, pushing the limits of range, data volume, and environmental interference, only to find the connection remained unyielding.


The implications were staggering. The security was absolute, the cost negligible compared to existing data networks, and the ease of deployment revolutionary, requiring no vast infrastructure beyond the two QEO devices themselves, which they had dubbed Ionics. Corvin recognized the profound societal implications immediately.


“This,” Corvin declared, holding one of the Ionics, “changes everything.”


They presented their collective inventions to the Public Works and Discovery council at the Temple of Physics. Zandil, surprisingly composed for the formal presentation, lectured with an air of dignified brilliance, occasionally interrupted by Corvin’s quiet, logical clarifications, which helped ground Zandil’s more abstract leaps.


Kael, though present, remained in the background, shrouded in a long cloak and tunic. A silent, unassuming presence, he absorbed every reaction, every whispered comment, trying to stay unnoticed in fear that the council members would take note of his age and question his birthright. His father, Corvin, expertly navigated the political landscape, explaining the practical applications and the immediate benefits to Veridia in terms that the council, initially skeptical after Zandil’s earlier dramatic pronouncements, could not deny. The QEOs and Ionics worked flawlessly.


Within a year of the official validation, the “Ionics” became ubiquitous. Most homes in Veridia had a compact, elegant Ionic terminal, allowing instant, secure communication and data access previously unimaginable. Government agencies relied on them for secure transmissions of sensitive directives and strategic intelligence, bypassing vulnerable networks. Scientists enjoyed instantaneous global collaboration, sharing experimental results and observations in real-time. Medical professionals transmitted vital patient data and diagnoses across distances, enabling faster, more accurate remote consultations, even guiding complex surgical procedures with live data feeds. Supply chains became hyper-efficient, reacting instantly to demand signals from across the city, minimizing waste. Educational institutions offered instant access to vast, interactive libraries and remote learning materials, democratizing knowledge like never before. The very pulse of Veridia quickened, subtly yet profoundly, all because of an eccentric genius, a dedicated engineer, and a boy who saw what no one else could.


Kael watched it all unfold from the periphery. He couldn’t take any share of the public credit, his birth still a closely guarded secret, but seeing the Ionic devices in every home, knowing the impact they had on countless lives, filled him with a quiet, profound satisfaction. His father was hailed as a co-inventor alongside the now-revered Dr. Zandil, and Kael felt no jealousy.


He was simply happy to have helped make Veridia a better, more connected world. A world that, in its newfound connectivity, might finally reach the goal of saving the people of this world.


And even more importantly to him, now that he had reached adulthood, he could roam the city with impunity.


Freedom was finally his.

It was a complex symphony of sensors and processors, tuned to detect the minute positive charge applied to one QEO and the instantly correlated negative charge on its twin. Kael obsessed over its efficiency, its speed, its capacity to translate the incredibly subtle “language” of entangled particles into coherent, usable information. He often worked late into the day, long after his father had retired, driven by an inner fire that made sleep seem like a waste of precious time.


Finally, after months of relentless refinement, the prototypes were ready. Two sleek, briefcase-sized devices, their polished weave-steel shells hiding the intricate quantum mechanisms within.


They could now transmit an entire textbook’s worth of data in the blink of an eye, powered by nothing more than a small energy pulse to initiate the link. They demonstrated not just text and images, but complex schematics, 3D models, and even perfectly replicated spoken words, streamed instantly and clearly across any distance within their simulated parameters. The team ran every conceivable test, pushing the limits of range, data volume, and environmental interference, only to find the connection remained unyielding.


The implications were staggering. The security was absolute, the cost negligible compared to existing data networks, and the ease of deployment revolutionary, requiring no vast infrastructure beyond the two QEO devices themselves, which they had dubbed Ionics. Corvin recognized the profound societal implications immediately.


“This,” Corvin declared, holding one of the Ionics, “changes everything.”


They presented their collective inventions to the Public Works and Discovery council at the Temple of Physics. Zandil, surprisingly composed for the formal presentation, lectured with an air of dignified brilliance, occasionally interrupted by Corvin’s quiet, logical clarifications, which helped ground Zandil’s more abstract leaps.


Kael, though present, remained in the background, shrouded in a long cloak and tunic. A silent, unassuming presence, he absorbed every reaction, every whispered comment, trying to stay unnoticed in fear that the council members would take note of his age and question his birthright. His father, Corvin, expertly navigated the political landscape, explaining the practical applications and the immediate benefits to Veridia in terms that the council, initially skeptical after Zandil’s earlier dramatic pronouncements, could not deny. The QEOs and Ionics worked flawlessly.


Within a year of the official validation, the “Ionics” became ubiquitous. Most homes in Veridia had a compact, elegant Ionic terminal, allowing instant, secure communication and data access previously unimaginable. Government agencies relied on them for secure transmissions of sensitive directives and strategic intelligence, bypassing vulnerable networks. Scientists enjoyed instantaneous global collaboration, sharing experimental results and observations in real-time. Medical professionals transmitted vital patient data and diagnoses across distances, enabling faster, more accurate remote consultations, even guiding complex surgical procedures with live data feeds. Supply chains became hyper-efficient, reacting instantly to demand signals from across the city, minimizing waste. Educational institutions offered instant access to vast, interactive libraries and remote learning materials, democratizing knowledge like never before. The very pulse of Veridia quickened, subtly yet profoundly, all because of an eccentric genius, a dedicated engineer, and a boy who saw what no one else could.


Kael watched it all unfold from the periphery. He couldn’t take any share of the public credit, his birth still a closely guarded secret, but seeing the Ionic devices in every home, knowing the impact they had on countless lives, filled him with a quiet, profound satisfaction. His father was hailed as a co-inventor alongside the now-revered Dr. Zandil, and Kael felt no jealousy.


He was simply happy to have helped make Veridia a better, more connected world. A world that, in its newfound connectivity, might finally reach the goal of saving the people of this world.


And even more importantly to him, now that he had reached adulthood, he could roam the city with impunity.


Freedom was finally his.

Chapter 8 - Non-Local Non-Zero


Hours earlier, the hum of the climate control was the only sound in the Solan-Vira apartment as Corvin and Varyn waited. They did not speak; they merely watched the utility access hatch.


When Kael finally slipped back through, silent as a ghost, Varyn was instantly by his side. She checked his breathing, his pulse, her worry momentarily eclipsing her training.


“I’m fine, Mom,” Kael whispered, his voice thin with exhaustion. He immediately unstrapped the matte-black Signal Shifter and handed it to Corvin. “It’s false, Dad. Structural Integrity: FALSE.”


Corvin took the device, his hands trembling slightly as he plugged it into his secure terminal. Varyn watched the screen, her heart in her throat. The screen displayed the raw data feed, a string of complex figures, ending with the single, damning Boolean Output: 0.


“The Equatorial Joint,” Corvin breathed, reading the coordinates. “The stress point. It’s degrading.”


Varyn sank onto the couch, running a hand through her hair. “A crack. A hairline fracture. It’s microscopic, but it’s there. The city has a breach.”


“It’s worse than a breach, Varyn,” Corvin said, his voice flat with horror. “If it’s degrading, it means the Nanoweave is compromised. The shield is failing at its most critical point. We have to monitor the growth of that crack.”


“But Kael just proved we can’t,” Varyn countered desperately. “Any scan from the main network will be instantly flagged by the Vigilae Sentinels. They’ll swarm the area.”


Corvin stared at the zero on the screen, a number that represented the death of their city. “We need eyes on that crack, Varyn, with continuous monitoring of the quantum state of the structure around it. But we can’t use light, energy, or radio. It would expose us.”


They worked in tense silence for the next few hours, attempting to devise a way to run a silent, invisible diagnostic. They failed. Every possible solution required a resource or authority they did not possess, or a system that the Vigilae would immediately trace.


The sun had long since risen and set again when the family finally collapsed into the living area, the weight of their secret pressing down on them.


At exactly 0200, the Solan apartment was wrapped in the deep, artificial quiet of Veridia’s recharge cycle. Corvin and Varyn watched with agonizing silence as Kael, matte-black **Signal Shifter** strapped to his small wrist, slipped out of the apartment through the utility access hatch. The airlock hissed shut, separating him from their desperate hope.


Kael moved with an unnerving efficiency, guided by Rixie's pre-programmed optimal route data. The utility corridors were narrow, utilitarian spaces, normally silent, but they amplified every footfall. The air was cool and smelled of dry synth-dust and machine lubricant. The **Signal Shifter** around his wrist thrummed softly, mimicking the harmless energy signature of a waste-management drone, allowing him to pass under the occasional, slow-moving surveillance scanner without notice.


His destination, the **Gamma-9 Maintenance Hub**, was a ghost in the city's flawless machine. It was located deep in a desolate sector near the primary shield foundation, a relic left behind by a catastrophic system upgrade two decades prior.


Following the faint, rhythmic vibration of the old hydraulic rail lines, Kael finally reached the access door. It was a massive, scarred portal of void-steel, secured by an outdated, mechanical latch and a simple, two-factor code lock that Rixie had efficiently bypassed.


Kael slipped inside. The hub was cavernous, cold, and utterly dead. Dust, undisturbed for cycles, coated every obsolete console and every coiled length of thick, armored conduit. It was nothing like his father's bright, organized workshop; this was a tomb of forgotten technology.


He found the main console easily, a huge, semicircular bank of gauges and controls that were dark, unresponsive, and covered in a thick layer of fine, grey sediment. The air here was heavy, tinged with a faint, static odor.


Kael ignored the physical interface. He didn’t need the screen or the keyboard. He closed his eyes and reached out with the unique gift his parents had worked so hard to conceal.


He focused, pushing his consciousness past the surface data and into the machine's core. Immediately, he *felt* it: the **fatal logic loop**.


It wasn't data he saw; it was a rhythmic, frantic tension, like a high-pitched, damaged cymbal reverberating in an endless loop. The console was attempting to calculate an output for a non-existent input, trapped in a recursive cycle that had completely crippled the main interface. It was a kind of digital screaming that drove away the streamlined modern Vigilae code, which registered the noise as lethal corruption.


But for Kael, it was a sound he could manipulate.


He pushed his awareness deeper, gently guiding the loop’s energy flow. He was nudging a piece of mental architecture, not code. He didn’t stop the loop; he simply rerouted a tiny, negligible piece of its wasted computational power into a side channel.


This side channel, a remnant of the pre-Matrix diagnostic tools, was still hardwired to the **Equatorial Expansion Joint's** primary sensors.


*One question.* That’s all Corvin had asked for.


Kael fed the loop a single, precise instruction: **Structural Integrity Diagnostic. Joint 9-Alpha. Boolean Output.**


The logic loop screamed a little louder as it grudgingly diverted the energy. Kael held the pain steady, his small face contorted with effort. On a tiny, salvageable gauge near the bottom of the console, a single indicator light—one of the old, incandescent filament bulbs—flickered to life.


It was red.


**Structural Integrity: FALSE.**


Kael opened his eyes, the image of the frantic, broken machine receding. He was shaking. It was the hardest thing he had ever done with his gift. He quickly unstrapped the Signal Shifter and connected it via a salvaged cable to the console's long-dormant data port. The red light pulsed its grim message into the device.


He had the answer.


He put the Signal Shifter back on his wrist. Now, he just had to get home without being caught. He turned to the door, the heavy void-steel feeling miles away.


The moment the Info-Cast ended, Corvin’s professional detachment evaporated, replaced by the grim urgency of a man holding a secret that could destroy the city. He didn't wait for morning.


Varyn, sharing his unspoken fear, was already at the main terminal. She worked with a speed and focus that betrayed her surface calm, navigating the city's complex data matrix.


“The Temple of Physics announcement was deliberately vague,” Varyn murmured, her fingers flying across the holographic console. “They validated the effect but redacted the method and the source.”


“They're protecting their find,” Corvin stated, pacing the length of the apartment. “It’s exactly what I would do. But they can’t hide his location forever. He’s an ‘independent researcher’—that means he has a residence tied to a power draw, and a lab that uses anomalous energy signatures.”


Varyn nodded, pulling up a map of Veridia’s energy distribution grid. “I’m filtering for high-level, unsanctioned power spikes that correlate with the timeframe of the Temple’s validation test… specifically looking for Bismuth-Silicate Matrix usage. That crystal structure Zandil used is highly distinct.”


The map of the sprawling city was initially a solid grid of green light, representing stable, authorized energy use. Varyn’s filters stripped away the green, leaving the map dark except for a few flickering yellow anomalies—minor, authorized variations.


Then, a single, sharp red spike pulsed in the middle of the crowded Industrial Sector, a zone typically reserved for low-level manufacturing and reprocessing.


“There,” Varyn breathed, zooming in on the red signature. “A sustained, massive power draw that peaked exactly when the Vigilae unit was in his lab. It's not a factory. The output is chaotic—like someone firing a plasma cutter and a laser grid simultaneously.”


Corvin leaned over the terminal, his gaze intense. “Trace the address, Varyn. If he has the key to instantaneous, undetectable observation, he is the key to our survival. He is our only hope of monitoring that crack without triggering a city-wide Vigilae swarm.”


Within moments, the red spike resolved into a physical location: a large, idiosyncratic house tucked between two massive coolant reclamation towers.


Residence: Zandil Aethel. Industrial Sector. Grid 12, Sub-level B.


“It’s late, and he’s probably asleep after his big reveal,” Varyn said, her finger hovering over the transit scheduler.


“Then we wake him up,” Corvin said, his voice hard with resolve. He grabbed a simple, durable coat. “We don't go through the Transit Tube. We take a service vehicle. We need to be quick, we need to be silent, and we need to look like maintenance. We go now.”


Later that evening, the soft, ambient light of the simulated fireplace flickered across the polished void-steel walls. Kael, Corvin, and Varyn were gathered in the living area, the earlier tension of the data confirmation still thick in the air. A large, wall-mounted Vidsplay shimmered with the latest city-wide Info-Cast – Veridia’s nightly broadcast of events, news, updates, and lifestyle pieces projected as a dynamic, holographic display.


The current segment featured a serious-looking official in Temple of Physics robes standing before the crystalline spire. Behind him, holographic diagrams of light beams splitting and strange, interconnected particles pulsed in the air.


“...and thus, the Temple of Physics has confirmed an extraordinary, albeit controversial, discovery by an independent researcher,” he held up a data crystal sheet and squinted at it. “Zandil Aethel.” the official intoned, his voice carrying an air of cautious astonishment. “Preliminary findings suggest a previously unknown phenomenon involving subatomic particles, which appear to exhibit an instantaneous, correlated relationship across spatial distances. This has been tentatively termed ‘quantum entanglement’ by the discovering scientist. However, further research is being allocated to understand the full implications of this potentially paradigm-shifting discovery…”


Kael watched the holographic display, his brow furrowed in thought. Beside him, Varyn exchanged a skeptical glance with Corvin.


“Quantum entanglement,” Varyn murmured. “Instantaneous correlation… It sounds… made up.”


Corvin nodded slowly, the grim data from the Equatorial Joint burning in his mind. “Yes, and costly. The energy requirements alone for such a connection across any significant distance would be astronomical.”


But Kael’s attention was no longer on the official’s cautious pronouncements. His eyes were wide with a sudden flash of insight.


‘If the state of a particle could be instantaneously linked and transmitted, could anything else be affected? Or even transmitted?’ His mind began to race, the gears of his intellect turning rapidly. ‘If not just the state or the charge, could data, could information itself be moved in this way? Instantly?’


Kael’s world began to slip out of focus, and the light that engulfed his mind from almost externally projected ideas surged. Like his ability to feel the state of electronics and circuitry, he was filled with a sense that seemed to originate from outside, pushing its way into him, but still as much a part of him as his own skin. This sudden flash of knowledge, of insight, and awareness of possibilities, felt the same. It was like the arm of a giant being slapping him into awareness.


He was AWARE.


The holographic diagrams on the Info-Cast continued to swirl, but Kael was already miles away in his thoughts, the seed of a revolutionary idea taking root.


From far away, he could barely hear his mother asking him if he was alright.


Then suddenly, he was back in his body. The world snapped into sharp, tactile focus, and the bowl of snacks he’d been holding shattered on the floor, scattering crumbs and synth-nuts everywhere. Before Kael could even register the mess, Gus, ever efficient, was already walking over, extending an articulated arm with a miniature vacuum attachment.


“Kael, are you alright?” Varyn was by his side in an instant, her hand gently touching his arm, her expression etched with concern. Corvin was already moving to assist Gus, his gaze sharp with worry as he looked from the mess to his son. “What happened? You just… froze up there.”


Kael shook his head, the lingering echoes of the “awareness” still humming in his mind. “Yes, I’m fine,” he mumbled, a faint blush rising to his cheeks as Gus efficiently vacuumed the last of the synth-nuts. “I just... I had an idea.”


He looked from his mother’s concerned face to his father’s expectant one, then back to the Info-Cast, which was now showing a diagram listing the fine points of this radical discovery.


“This,” he paused to look back to his internal database of awareness, “quantum entanglement, they said that the particles share a coequal state of being, instantaneously. That if you observe one, the other changes. And that if you charge one, the other takes the same charge.” Kael’s eyes brightened, losing focus as the excitement of the idea took over. “But what if... what if it was more than just state or charge? What if it were information? If you could impress a pattern, a sequence of data, onto one particle, would the other instantly reflect that data, no matter how far apart they were?”


Corvin let out a slow whistle in amazement and understanding.


Varyn frowned, doubtful but intrigued. “Information transfer? Without a conduit? That’s... impossible, Kael. As your father said, the energy required to encode and transmit even a single byte through such a method would be insurmountable.”


“But Zandil didn’t say the energy was excessive in his demonstration,” Kael countered, turning to his father. “He used an old laser grid and a salvaged crystal. He put it together with scraps he salvaged from junk piles and discarded tech. So he must have figured out a way around that. What if the energy isn’t required for transmission, but just for linking? Once they’re linked, the information just... is.” He paused, his gaze sweeping over his parents. “Imagine. If we could transmit data instantly, across any distance, without a beam or a fiber. No lag. No interference. Maybe we could build a communication device that doesn’t rely on light speed?”


Corvin’s initial skepticism began to soften, replaced by a glint of the same intellectual hunger that drove his own innovations. Instantaneous, undetectable data transfer wasn’t a communications project; it was the only way to monitor the fatal crack in the shield.


Finally, Corvin spoke, a thoughtful expression on his face. “Well. That could just be something. Varyn, do any of your contacts in the bio-science division have connections in physics? Maybe we could find out where this Zandil lives and pay him a friendly visit.” He smiled at his family of brilliant minds.


“I don’t know. I’ll ask around tomorrow,” Varyn said, rising from the soft couch with a stretch and a loud yawn. “But for now, it’s late. Let’s hit the tube.” She walked towards the stairs. “Good night, starlight. Love you.”


“Love you too, Mom,” Kael called to her retreating shadow.


Soon, the apartment was quiet. Kael, Corvin, and Varyn each entered their personal sleep tubes. Kael waited a little while longer before closing his lid, hoping that a flash of awareness would hit him again, but the silence remained unbroken.


Only the faint hum of Rixie’s charging port outside his door filled the room.


He closed the lid and was fast asleep.

Corvin’s initial skepticism began to soften, replaced by a glint of the same intellectual hunger that drove his own innovations. Instantaneous, undetectable data transfer wasn’t a communications project; it was the only way to monitor the fatal crack in the shield.


Finally, Corvin spoke, a thoughtful expression on his face. “Well. That could just be something. Varyn, do any of your contacts in the bio-science division have connections in physics? Maybe we could find out where this Zandil lives and pay him a friendly visit.” He smiled at his family of brilliant minds.


“I don’t know. I’ll ask around tomorrow,” Varyn said, rising from the soft couch with a stretch and a loud yawn. “But for now, it’s late. Let’s hit the tube.” She walked towards the stairs. “Good night, starlight. Love you.”


“Love you too, Mom,” Kael called to her retreating shadow.


Soon, the apartment was quiet. Kael, Corvin, and Varyn each entered their personal sleep tubes. Kael waited a little while longer before closing his lid, hoping that a flash of awareness would hit him again, but the silence remained unbroken.


Only the faint hum of Rixie’s charging port outside his door filled the room.


He closed the lid and was fast asleep.
